title: Cannot shadow volume subtree via a tmpfs mount
body:
### Issue Description

A tmpfs mounted inside the mounted tree of a persistent filesystem never occludes the subtree under its own mount point. The parent filesystem remains fully visible regardless of argv order.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
```shell
$ podman volume create repro  # or use a directory on the host filesystem
$ podman run --rm --volume repro:/data alpine sh -c 'mkdir -p /data/sub && echo hello > /data/sub/file'
$ podman run --rm --volume repro:/data --tmpfs /data/sub alpine sh -c 'cat /data/sub/file'
hello
$ podman run --rm --tmpfs /data/sub --volume repro:/data alpine sh -c 'cat /data/sub/file'
hello
```

### Describe the results you received

The canary file is always accessible by path. The `--tmpfs` mount is always the one shadowed by its parent.

### Describe the results you expected

The canary file should be shadowed, and trying to read it should fail with
```shell
cat: can't open '/data/sub/file': No such file or directory
```
This should be the case either in both call orders (mounts automatically ordered by specificity) or at least in the first one (mounts created according to argv order).

### podman info output

comments.body:
It seems that these lines set the default `tmpcopyup`. https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/18acc613d0d18ce922730d9632a773a3d1885dec/pkg/util/mount_opts.go#L221-L222 

You can work around that with:
```
podman run --rm --volume repro:/data --mount type=tmpfs,dst=/data/sub,notmpcopyup alpine sh -c 'cat /data/sub/file'
```

To solve this, I would detect any tmpfs mount whose destination is a child of another user-specified mount (volume or bind) and inject `notmpcopyup` into its options so `ProcessOptions` won't add the default `tmpcopyup`. WDYT? @giuseppe

that makes sense to me.

What does Docker do in this case?  Does it skip the tmpcopyup?
I am not sure I will test that.

> To solve this, I would detect any tmpfs mount whose destination is a child of another user-specified mount (volume or bind) and inject notmpcopyup into its options so ProcessOptions won't add the default tmpcopyup. WDYT? @giuseppe 


Why though? Special cases make the code just much harder to reason about for both the devs and end users.

The default of tmpfs is copyup so it does exactly that, if users do not want that they can just disable that via the option. Saying by default we copy up but then not when the source is a volume is confusing because if the source is a volume is actually not easily visible to users. It is entirely possible that the image has a volume declared at the parent dir making the path a volume without it being visable from the cli, same for volume paths injected via config file and so on.

I agree it is worth to see how docker behaves but overall I like us keeping the current behavior.


> --mount type=tmpfs,dst=/data/sub,notmpcopyup

--tmpfs /tmp:notmpcopyup works as well if using --mount is not wanted

Docker has different behavior:
```
$ docker volume create repro
repro
$ docker run --rm --volume repro:/data alpine sh -c 'mkdir -p /data/sub && echo hello > /data/sub/file'
$ docker run --rm --volume repro:/data --tmpfs /data/sub alpine sh -c 'cat /data/sub/file'
cat: can't open '/data/sub/file': No such file or directory
$ docker run --rm --tmpfs /data/sub --volume repro:/data alpine sh -c 'cat /data/sub/file'
cat: can't open '/data/sub/file': No such file or directory
```

So, should we match Docker's behavior or keep our current behavior? @giuseppe @Luap99

docker does not actually default to tmpcopyup though? https://docs.docker.com/engine/storage/tmpfs/#mounting-over-existing-data

```
root@localhost:~# docker run --rm --tmpfs /etc alpine ls /etc
hostname
hosts
resolv.conf
root@localhost:~# docker run --rm alpine ls /etc
alpine-release
apk
busybox-paths.d
crontabs
...
```

So the behavior is the same regardless of the parent mount or not. And it seems docker does not understand `tmpcopyup`/`notmpcopyup` at all so users can not switch this there.

So I guess the question would be should we default to notmpcopyup? What is the history of podman choosing tmpcopyup as default? With 6.0 we may be able to switch this right now.

https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/4e5e9dbec2313b07a4c10ddfd5bc7d23e3fa34f6 is very old so podman basically always behaved like that. Given this is the first time I hear about it I don't think we should just chnage just for one issue. We could have many people depending on the current default.

If I may offer my perspective

Coming over from docker, this was unexpected for me. I didn't know about the `no/tmpcopyup` flags and wouldn't have guessed podman defaults to `tmpcopyup`, which makes tmpfs mounts present different behavior from other mount types. When I noticed this behavior I kind of assumed it was a problem with mounting order accidentally always shadowing the tmpfs mount. This is the first occasion I encountered where I find docker's defaults to be preferable.

Still, while I have my doubts about the choice, I wouldn't consider this in itself reason enough to break compatibility, especially when it has persisted for years with no comment until now. But I do think two points need to be weighed here.

First, the docker userbase is large and relevant. This incompatibility is a hindrance to adoption. I didn't expect it, and AI agents, which receive most of their containerization training data from examples of docker usage, don't seem to expect it either. I now see that the documentation does explicitly state this is the default, but if a new user doesn't know to refer to this part of the documentation…
Second, tmpfs mounts over parent mounts are a common pattern for masking data, e.g. "keep configuration synchronized but mask login credentials". With the current default, a tmpfs mount meant to mask sensitive data instead silently exposes it — to code that was placed in a container specifically because it isn't trusted. And even in the benign case, application code can happily pick up the unmasked content and behave in unintended ways. In my case it manifested as a runtime failure, which I'd argue is the lucky outcome.

Breaking compatibility is bad. But so is leaving footguns — and possible security risks — in place. Both options have unpalatable consequences.

This discussion already gave me workable solutions for my own use — I'll just always explicitly set the flag as appropriate. And I certainly agree that simply flipping the default would not be a good idea at this point. But longer-term, it might be worth starting a deprecation cycle to change it.

Another point to keep in mind, whether it is the right default or not, is that `tmpcopyup` could be an expensive operation both at startup and at runtime.  It copies whatever it finds at the underlying path into memory.
I am somewhere between. If we want to change the default, podman 6 is a good opportunity. WDYT? @containers/podman-maintainers

I think fairly strongly we shouldn’t have a special case depending on the parent.

As for the default behavior, I’m leaning towards preserving the long-standing Podman behavior, but I don’t feel that strongly.

It appears we'll keep the current `tmpcopyup` default. I've opened a PR to improve the documentation for both `--tmpfs` and `--mount type=tmpfs` to explicitly highlight this behavior and the workaround. 
 
- #28709
